covid_lungs: Lungs with bilateral ground-glass opacities and peripheral consolidation, mimicking viral pneumonia but caused by acute hypersensitivity pneumonitis with upper lobe predominance.
Diffuse interstitial thickening and subpleural reticulation resembling organizing pneumonia, lacking the vascular enlargement seen in true COVID-19 pathology.
Central bronchiectasis with mucoid impaction and tree-in-bud opacities, simulating early viral infection but indicative of chronic aspiration or cystic fibrosis.
Unilateral pleural effusion with overlying atelectasis and mediastinal shift, mimicking severe airspace disease but secondary to heart failure or trauma.
Nodular calcifications in the lower lobes with adjacent fibrosis, resembling chronic fibrosing interstitial pneumonia rather than the acute inflammatory response of SARS-CoV-2.
lung_opacity_lungs: Central perihilar opacities with a "bat wing" distribution, often seen in congestive heart failure, mimicking bilateral lung involvement without peripheral sparing.
Linear, plate-like densities at lung bases due to collapsed alveoli, mistaken for consolidation despite maintaining air bronchograms and lacking surrounding inflammation.
Basilar opacities with indistinct costophrenic angles caused by pleural fluid accumulation, closely resembling lower zone pneumonia or lung opacity.
Diffuse, hazy lucencies with poor margin definition caused by patient motion during image acquisition, simulating ground-glass opacities seen in pulmonary edema.
Increased density observed in lower lung fields caused by overlying abdominal contents due to elevated diaphragms, closely mimicking basal consolidations.
normal_lungs: Mild interstitial pneumonia with faint peripheral reticular opacities resembling normal vascular patterns, minimal septal thickening, and absence of consolidations mimicking healthy lung parenchyma.
Early pulmonary edema presenting with subtle hazy ground-glass attenuation in lower lobes, indistinct vessel margins, and minimal Kerley B lines resembling normal lung texture in low-dose imaging.

Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) with hyperinflated lungs, flattened diaphragms, and scattered low-attenuation areas resembling normal lung lucency despite underlying emphysematous changes.
Post-viral fibrosis with fine reticular markings in peripheral zones, mild traction bronchiectasis, and preserved lung volume resembling normal bronchovascular anatomy without acute pathology.
Lung contusion from trauma showing patchy, poorly defined areas of increased opacity with faint air bronchograms, mimicking normal lung markings in subacute phases without overt consolidation.
viral_pneumonia_lungs: Dense lobar consolidation with air bronchograms and sharp margins, typically seen in bacterial pneumonia, mimicking viral pneumonia's opacity patterns but localized to single lobes.
Diffuse ground-glass opacities with superimposed cystic lesions and upper lobe predominance, resembling fungal infections like Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia rather than viral etiology.
Upper lobe fibrosis with cavitation and hilar lymphadenopathy, visually overlapping with tuberculosis but distinct from viral pneumonia's peripheral infiltrates.
Subpleural reticular opacities with honeycombing and traction bronchiectasis, indicative of interstitial lung disease rather than the ground-glass or consolidative patterns of viral pneumonia.
Bilateral perihilar "bat-wing" opacities with Kerley B lines and pleural effusions, reflecting pulmonary edema's vascular redistribution rather than viral pneumonia's parenchymal involvement.
